events.text: The patient is a 48-year-old male named Marcus T. Delgado. He works as a high school science teacher and is a non-smoker with no significant alcohol use. He has a history of well-controlled hypertension managed with lisinopril. Family history is notable for a maternal uncle with colon cancer diagnosed at age 62. He has no prior hematologic conditions.

At age 48 years, the patient was diagnosed with Acute Myeloid Leukemia (AML), NOS, arising de novo. AJCC staging not applicable; AML classified per WHO 2022 criteria. Primary site: bone marrow and peripheral blood (hematopoietic system). ICD-O-3 site code: C42.1 (bone marrow). Histology: Acute myeloid leukemia without maturation, ICD-O-3 morphology code 9861/3. FAB classification: M1 (AML without maturation). Presenting white blood cell count: 84,000/uL (leukocytosis), hemoglobin 7.2 g/dL (anemia), platelets 28,000/uL (thrombocytopenia). Peripheral blood blast percentage: 62%. Bone marrow biopsy blast percentage: 78%. LDH elevated at 1,240 U/L. Presenting symptoms included 3 weeks of progressive fatigue, pallor, easy bruising, and gingival hypertrophy with spontaneous bleeding. Cytogenetics: 46,XY, normal karyotype (diploid). ELN 2022 risk classification: Adverse risk due to FLT3-ITD with high allelic ratio (AR 0.87). Confirmation: Positive histology with flow cytometry immunophenotyping. Flow cytometry immunophenotype: CD34+, CD117+, CD13+, CD33+, HLA-DR+, MPO+, CD14-, CD64-, CD19-, CD3-, consistent with AML. No Auer rods identified on peripheral smear.

At age 48, the patient underwent a bilateral bone marrow biopsy and aspirate for initial staging and diagnosis. Specimen: bilateral posterior iliac crest core biopsies and aspirate smears. Gross: bilateral cores each measuring 1.8 cm in length, adequate cellularity. Microscopic: marrow is markedly hypercellular (estimated 90-95% cellularity) with replacement by a monotonous proliferation of immature myeloid blasts. Blasts are medium-to-large with high nuclear-to-cytoplasmic ratio, fine chromatin, and one to three prominent nucleoli. Erythropoiesis and megakaryopoiesis are markedly suppressed. No fibrosis on reticulin stain. Immunohistochemistry: blasts positive for CD34, CD117, MPO; negative for CD3, CD20, CD64, TdT. Diagnosis: Acute myeloid leukemia, involving greater than 78% of marrow cellularity. Cytogenetics (conventional G-band karyotype from aspirate): 46,XY [20/20 metaphases analyzed], normal male karyotype.

At age 48, the patient had next-generation sequencing (NGS) performed for AML based on a specimen obtained from bone marrow aspirate at diagnosis. A comprehensive myeloid NGS panel (283-gene panel) was used. Clinically significant findings: FLT3 internal tandem duplication (ITD) at exon 14-15 junction, allelic ratio 0.87 (high), VAF 47%; NPM1 wild-type (negative); DNMT3A p.R882H mutation, VAF 42%; IDH1 wild-type; IDH2 wild-type; TET2 wild-type; ASXL1 wild-type; TP53 wild-type. Additional findings of uncertain significance: PTPN11 p.E76K mutation, VAF 18% (likely leukemic subclone). Copy number alterations: no significant copy number alterations detected. Fusions: FLT3-ITD confirmed; no FLT3 TKD (D835) mutation; no KMT2A rearrangement; no RUNX1-RUNX1T1; no CBFB-MYH11; no PML-RARA; no BCR-ABL1. CEBPA: wild-type. RUNX1: wild-type. WT1: wild-type. NRAS: wild-type. KRAS: wild-type. KIT: wild-type. JAK2: wild-type. EZH2: wild-type. BCOR: wild-type. STAG2: wild-type. Summary: AML with FLT3-ITD (high allelic ratio, AR 0.87) and DNMT3A R882H co-mutation; ELN 2022 adverse-risk disease. Allogeneic transplant in first remission strongly recommended. Actionable alterations: FLT3-ITD (targetable with midostaurin in induction, sorafenib/gilteritinib post-transplant maintenance to be considered).

At age 48, the patient had a CT scan of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis with IV contrast performed as part of pre-treatment staging workup. Findings: No mediastinal, hilar, or axillary lymphadenopathy. Lungs are clear without parenchymal consolidation, nodule, or pleural effusion. Liver: mild hepatomegaly (liver span 17.2 cm craniocaudal), no focal hepatic lesions. Spleen: mild splenomegaly (spleen measures 14.1 cm in longest axis), homogeneous enhancement, no splenic lesions. No retroperitoneal or mesenteric lymphadenopathy. Kidneys: bilateral symmetric enhancement, no masses. No pelvic lymphadenopathy. Bones: diffuse marrow signal abnormality, compatible with marrow infiltration by leukemia. No lytic or blastic osseous lesions. Impression: Hepatosplenomegaly consistent with systemic disease involvement. No evidence of chloroma (granulocytic sarcoma) or extramedullary AML. Cancer status: cancer present, no discrete metastatic lesions, hepatosplenomegaly consistent with leukemic involvement.

At age 48, the patient was admitted to the inpatient hematology/oncology service for initiation of induction chemotherapy. Performance status: ECOG 1. The patient presented with a 3-week history of progressive fatigue, pallor, gingival hypertrophy with spontaneous bleeding, and occasional epistaxis. Physical examination: temperature 37.1 C, BP 138/84 mmHg, HR 102 bpm, RR 16, O2 saturation 98% on room air. Mild pallor. Oral mucosa with gingival hypertrophy and friable tissue with minimal ooze. No petechiae on palate. Spleen palpable 3 cm below costal margin. No lymphadenopathy. Neurologic exam intact. No skin infiltration. Echocardiogram performed: ejection fraction 62%, no wall motion abnormality, suitable for anthracycline-based therapy. Lumbar puncture performed: CSF cytology negative for blasts, no CNS involvement. Diagnosis confirmed: de novo AML with FLT3-ITD high allelic ratio, ELN adverse risk. Plan: Initiate 7+3 induction with cytarabine 100 mg/m2/day continuous infusion days 1-7 plus daunorubicin 60 mg/m2/day IV bolus days 1, 2, 3, with midostaurin 50 mg PO twice daily days 8-21. Allopurinol and IV hydration started for tumor lysis prophylaxis. HLA typing submitted for unrelated donor search.

At age 48, the patient received curative-intent induction chemotherapy with cytarabine 100 mg/m2/day continuous IV infusion days 1 through 7 (7+3 regimen), daunorubicin 60 mg/m2 IV bolus days 1, 2, and 3, and midostaurin 50 mg orally twice daily days 8 through 21. This was cycle 1, induction cycle 1. Treatment was administered in the inpatient setting. Midostaurin was initiated as a FLT3 inhibitor per RATIFY trial protocol. Total cytarabine dose: 700 mg/m2 (body surface area 1.95 m2, actual doses rounded per institutional protocol). Central venous access: PICC line placed prior to initiation. Antiemetics: ondansetron and dexamethasone per protocol. Antimicrobial prophylaxis: fluconazole, levofloxacin, valacyclovir initiated.

At age 48, during induction chemotherapy (days 10-18 post-initiation), the patient developed febrile neutropenia. Absolute neutrophil count nadir: 0 cells/uL (day 14). Temperature 38.6 C. Blood cultures obtained (2 sets peripherally and from PICC line). Chest X-ray: no consolidation. Empiric broad-spectrum antibiotics started with cefepime 2g IV every 8 hours. Blood cultures ultimately negative. Fever resolved after 72 hours on antibiotics. Additionally, grade 2 oral mucositis developed (NCI CTCAE grade 2, moderate, requiring modified diet but not IV nutrition). Managed with magic mouthwash, palifermin was not available. Patient also experienced grade 2 nausea and vomiting, managed with scheduled ondansetron and as-needed lorazepam. Grade 1 alopecia noted. Midostaurin was temporarily held for 5 days due to nausea/vomiting and then restarted at same dose. No cardiotoxicity. No hepatotoxicity. ANC recovery to greater than 1000/uL occurred on day 28.

At age 48 (approximately day 30 post-induction initiation), the patient had a CT scan of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis with IV contrast performed as a response assessment study. Findings: Liver size now normal (craniocaudal span 14.8 cm). Spleen has returned to normal size (9.4 cm). No lymphadenopathy. No pleural effusion. No new pulmonary infiltrates. Osseous structures: no focal lytic or blastic lesions. No evidence of extramedullary disease. Impression: Resolution of prior hepatosplenomegaly. No evidence of persistent extramedullary AML. Cancer status: imaging response to therapy, hepatosplenomegaly resolved.

At age 48 (day 28-30 post-induction, remission assessment bone marrow biopsy), the patient underwent bilateral posterior iliac crest bone marrow biopsy and aspirate for response assessment. Gross: two adequate core biopsies each measuring 1.5 cm. Microscopic: marrow is markedly hypocellular (estimated 5-10% cellularity), consistent with post-chemotherapy aplasia. Trilineage hematopoiesis is recovering with early erythroid and myeloid precursors present. No morphologically identifiable blasts. No dysplastic changes. Megakaryocytes present and morphologically unremarkable. Reticulin stain: grade 0-1 fibrosis. Immunohistochemistry: CD34 stain highlights rare cells consistent with normal progenitors (less than 1%). No aberrant CD34/CD117 co-expression. Cytogenetics: 46,XY [20/20 metaphases], normal male karyotype. Molecular: PCR for FLT3-ITD: NEGATIVE. Diagnosis: AML in complete morphologic remission (CR1), post-induction bone marrow. Blast count less than 5% by morphology. Trilineage hematopoietic recovery ongoing.

At age 48 (approximately day 30-32 post-induction), the patient was seen in the outpatient hematology/oncology clinic for remission assessment. CBC: WBC 3.8 k/uL, ANC 1,820/uL, hemoglobin 10.4 g/dL, platelets 82,000/uL (count recovering). Patient reports feeling significantly better; fatigue improved, gingival bleeding resolved, appetite returning. Physical examination: No hepatosplenomegaly. No lymphadenopathy. Oral mucosa: healed, no mucositis. ECOG 1 (mild fatigue). Bone marrow biopsy results reviewed: complete morphologic remission confirmed. MRD assessment by multiparameter flow cytometry (10-color, sensitivity 10^-4) performed on aspirate: NEGATIVE (no residual leukemia-associated immunophenotype detected, less than 0.01% blasts). Assessment: AML in CR1, MRD-negative by flow cytometry after first induction. Cancer absent. Given ELN adverse risk (FLT3-ITD high allelic ratio, DNMT3A R882H), allogeneic stem cell transplant in CR1 is strongly indicated. HLA typing results reviewed; patient is HLA-A, B, C, DRB1, DQB1 typed (10/10). Unrelated donor search in progress through NMDP. Plan: Proceed with matched unrelated donor (MUD) allogeneic hematopoietic stem cell transplantation (allo-HSCT) in CR1. No consolidation chemotherapy planned prior to transplant (to avoid delay given MRD negativity and donor availability). Bridge to transplant: midostaurin continuation considered but held pending transplant center recommendation.

At age 48 (approximately 6 weeks post-induction), the patient attended a pre-transplant evaluation at the transplant center. Evaluation included cardiac assessment (repeat echocardiogram: EF 60%, normal), pulmonary function tests (FEV1 88% predicted, DLCO 82% predicted, adequate for transplant), renal function (creatinine 0.9 mg/dL, GFR >80), hepatic function (AST/ALT within normal limits, total bilirubin 0.7 mg/dL), and infectious disease screening (CMV IgG seropositive, EBV seropositive, HSV seropositive, VZV seropositive, HIV negative, Hepatitis B surface antigen negative, Hepatitis C antibody negative). Dental evaluation: completed, no active dental infections. Donor: 10/10 HLA-matched unrelated donor identified through NMDP (male, CMV seropositive). Performance status: KPS 80. Transplant plan: myeloablative conditioning with fludarabine 30 mg/m2 days -6 through -2 and busulfan IV 3.2 mg/kg days -5 through -2 (FluBu4 regimen). GVHD prophylaxis: tacrolimus plus methotrexate (short course). No in vivo T-cell depletion. Admission planned in 2 weeks.

At age 48 (approximately 9 weeks after initial AML diagnosis), the patient received myeloablative conditioning chemotherapy for allogeneic stem cell transplant, consisting of fludarabine 30 mg/m2 IV daily on days -6, -5, -4, -3, and -2 (total 5 days), and busulfan 3.2 mg/kg IV daily on days -5, -4, -3, and -2 (total 4 days, FluBu4 regimen). Busulfan pharmacokinetic monitoring performed; dose adjustments made to achieve target AUC of 5000-6000 uM*min/day. Seizure prophylaxis with levetiracetam administered throughout busulfan dosing. Concurrent antimicrobial prophylaxis: fluconazole, acyclovir, and trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole. GVHD prophylaxis: tacrolimus started day -3, target trough 10-15 ng/mL; methotrexate 15 mg/m2 IV day +1 and 10 mg/m2 IV days +3, +6, +11 (short-course MTX). G-CSF-mobilized peripheral blood stem cells from 10/10 MUD infused on day 0, total CD34+ cell dose 5.8 x 10^6/kg.

At age 48, during the peri-transplant period (days 0 through +30), the patient experienced several expected complications. Febrile neutropenia developed on day +8 (ANC nadir 0, temperature 38.8 C); empiric cefepime started; blood cultures negative; fever resolved in 48 hours. Grade 2 oral mucositis (NCI CTCAE) developed on day +6, requiring IV morphine PCA for pain management and nutritional support with supplemental tube feeds for 8 days. Engraftment: neutrophil engraftment (ANC >500/uL for 3 consecutive days) achieved on day +14; platelet engraftment (platelets >20,000/uL without transfusion) on day +19. Grade 1 nausea throughout conditioning, managed with ondansetron. No sinusoidal obstruction syndrome (SOS/VOD): total bilirubin remained less than 2 mg/dL, no weight gain, no right upper quadrant pain. Tacrolimus-related tremors: mild grade 1, dose adjusted. Creatinine mildly elevated (1.4 mg/dL, grade 1 nephrotoxicity) attributed to tacrolimus; managed with dose reduction.

At age 48 (day +30 post-transplant), the patient underwent bone marrow biopsy and aspirate for engraftment assessment. Microscopic: marrow is hypercellular to normocellular (60-70% cellularity), with trilineage hematopoiesis. Erythroid, myeloid, and megakaryocytic lineages all present and maturing appropriately. No morphologic evidence of residual AML (blasts less than 5% by morphology, less than 1% estimated). Reticulin stain: minimal fibrosis (grade 0-1). Immunohistochemistry: CD34 highlights only rare progenitors, no aberrant blast clusters. Chimerism studies (STR-based donor/recipient chimerism): whole blood chimerism 97% donor (3% recipient); CD3+ T-cell chimerism 91% donor; myeloid (CD33+) chimerism 99% donor. FLT3-ITD by PCR: NEGATIVE. Diagnosis: Post-allogeneic transplant bone marrow with trilineage engraftment, no evidence of residual or recurrent AML, near-complete donor chimerism.

At age 48 (day +45 post-transplant), the patient was seen in the outpatient BMT clinic. CBC: WBC 5.2 k/uL, ANC 3,100/uL, hemoglobin 11.8 g/dL, platelets 142,000/uL. Patient reports fatigue (ECOG 1), mild dry mouth, and mild skin dryness of bilateral hands. No fever. No diarrhea. Physical examination: skin of bilateral palms shows mild erythema and scaling, consistent with grade 1 chronic GVHD (skin). Oral mucosa: mild erythema and mild lichenoid changes of buccal mucosa, grade 1 oral chronic GVHD. No sclerodermatous changes. No hepatomegaly. No ocular symptoms. Global GVHD score: mild chronic GVHD (NIH consensus criteria). Assessment: AML in ongoing remission post-MUD allo-HSCT day +45. Mild chronic GVHD affecting skin and oral mucosa. Cancer absent. Plan: Continue tacrolimus (trough 8 ng/mL, beginning taper). Initiate low-potency topical corticosteroids (triamcinolone 0.1% ointment) for skin GVHD. Magic mouthwash and topical budesonide oral rinse for oral GVHD. Monitor LFTs, creatinine. Continue acyclovir, trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole, and fluconazole prophylaxis. FLT3-ITD molecular monitoring by PCR quarterly.

At age 48 (approximately day +60 to +120 post-transplant), the patient had ongoing mild chronic graft-versus-host disease (cGVHD) involving skin and oral mucosa, classified as mild per NIH 2014 consensus criteria. Skin score: 1 (less than 18% BSA with erythema, no sclerosis). Oral score: 1 (mild symptoms, able to eat). Liver function tests: AST 42 U/L (mild grade 1 elevation), ALT 48 U/L (mild grade 1 elevation), total bilirubin 0.9 mg/dL; consistent with possible early hepatic cGVHD contribution but not diagnostic. No gastrointestinal GVHD. No pulmonary GVHD (FEV1 stable at 91% predicted on repeat spirometry). No ocular GVHD. Management: Tacrolimus taper continued (target trough 5-8 ng/mL); topical therapies maintained. No systemic corticosteroids required. Patient tolerating well with functional improvement. ECOG performance status improved to 0-1.

At age 48 (day +90 post-transplant), the patient had a CT scan of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis with IV contrast performed as disease surveillance imaging. Findings: No mediastinal, hilar, axillary, or retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy. Lungs are clear; no consolidation, nodules, or pleural effusion. Liver: normal size (craniocaudal span 13.9 cm), no focal hepatic lesions, no biliary dilatation. Spleen: normal size (9.1 cm), homogeneous. No new osseous lesions. No ascites. No pelvic adenopathy. Impression: No evidence of AML recurrence. No extramedullary disease. Normal post-transplant CT appearance. Cancer status: no cancer detected, complete response maintained.

At age 48 (day +100 post-transplant), the patient was seen in the BMT long-term follow-up clinic. CBC: WBC 6.8 k/uL, ANC 4,200/uL, hemoglobin 13.1 g/dL, platelets 198,000/uL. Patient reports significant improvement in energy levels, return to part-time work as a teacher, and mild ongoing skin dryness of hands. Oral GVHD symptoms minimally improved. Physical examination: Mild residual lichenoid changes of bilateral buccal mucosa. Skin: erythema of bilateral palms resolved, mild residual scaling. No sclerodermatous changes. No ocular symptoms. Liver: not palpable. Spleen: not palpable. Assessment: AML in complete remission, day +100 post-MUD allo-HSCT. Mild chronic GVHD (skin and oral) stable and improving. Cancer absent. Tacrolimus taper progressing. Repeat FLT3-ITD PCR from peripheral blood: NEGATIVE. CMV PCR from peripheral blood: undetectable. EBV PCR: undetectable. Plan: Continue tacrolimus taper with goal discontinuation by month 6-9. Continue GVHD monitoring. Continue PCP prophylaxis, antiviral prophylaxis. Bone marrow biopsy planned at day +180 for formal remission confirmation and chimerism. Vaccination schedule to be initiated at 6 months post-transplant.

At age 48-49 (day +180 post-transplant), the patient underwent protocol bone marrow biopsy and aspirate for remission assessment. Microscopic: marrow is normocellular (50-60% cellularity), with full trilineage hematopoiesis. Erythroid islands present and maturing. Myeloid series shows full maturation. Megakaryocytes adequate in number and morphologically normal. Blasts less than 2% by morphology (within normal limits). No Auer rods. No dysplastic changes. Reticulin stain: grade 0 fibrosis. CD34 immunohistochemistry: scattered CD34+ progenitors approximately 1-2%, normal distribution. Chimerism (STR-based): whole blood chimerism 99% donor; CD3+ T-cell chimerism 96% donor; CD33+ myeloid chimerism 100% donor. FLT3-ITD PCR from marrow aspirate: NEGATIVE (sensitivity 10^-4). Multiparameter flow cytometry MRD: NEGATIVE (less than 0.01% blasts, no leukemia-associated immunophenotype). DNMT3A R882H digital droplet PCR: NEGATIVE. Diagnosis: AML in complete remission with MRD negativity, 6 months post-allogeneic transplant. Full donor chimerism achieved. No evidence of relapse.

At age 49 (approximately 9 months post-transplant), the patient was seen in the BMT outpatient clinic for routine surveillance. CBC: WBC 7.1 k/uL, ANC 4,600/uL, hemoglobin 13.8 g/dL, platelets 220,000/uL. Patient has returned to full-time teaching. Fatigue is minimal. Skin and oral GVHD: significantly improved. Oral mucosa: faint lichenoid changes remain on buccal mucosa bilaterally but asymptomatic. Skin: no active erythema or scaling. Tacrolimus successfully tapered and discontinued at month 8 post-transplant without flare of GVHD. Physical examination: ECOG 0. No lymphadenopathy. No organomegaly. Assessment: AML in sustained complete remission, 9 months post-MUD allo-HSCT. Mild chronic GVHD (skin and oral) improving and near resolution off immunosuppression. Cancer absent. FLT3-ITD PCR from peripheral blood: NEGATIVE. Plan: Ongoing surveillance with quarterly clinic visits and CBC. Annual bone marrow biopsy per protocol. Vaccination series initiated (inactivated influenza, pneumococcal, Tdap administered; live vaccines deferred to month 24). Sun protection and skin care counseling. Ongoing monitoring for late transplant complications (endocrine, bone density, secondary malignancy risk counseling). Tacrolimus discontinued.

At age 49 (approximately 12 months post-transplant), the patient had a CT scan of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis with IV contrast for one-year post-transplant surveillance. Findings: No lymphadenopathy in chest, abdomen, or pelvis. Lungs: clear, no pulmonary nodules, consolidation, or effusion. Cardiac silhouette normal. Liver: normal size and enhancement. Spleen: normal size (8.8 cm). Adrenal glands: normal. Kidneys: bilateral symmetric enhancement, no masses. No pelvic abnormalities. Osseous structures: no focal lytic or blastic lesions; diffuse marrow signal homogeneous, no evidence of marrow replacement. Impression: No evidence of AML recurrence or extramedullary disease at 12 months post-allogeneic transplant. Cancer status: no cancer detected, ongoing complete response.

At age 49 (approximately 12-13 months post-transplant), the patient was seen for annual follow-up visit. CBC: WBC 7.4 k/uL, ANC 5,100/uL, hemoglobin 14.0 g/dL, platelets 231,000/uL. All counts within normal limits. Patient is asymptomatic, fully active, and has resumed all normal activities including recreational sports. Chronic GVHD: skin and oral GVHD fully resolved. No new GVHD manifestations. No immunosuppression (tacrolimus discontinued at month 8). Physical examination: ECOG 0. Normal exam. Assessment: AML in complete remission at 12 months post-MUD allo-HSCT. Chronic GVHD fully resolved. No evidence of relapse. Cancer absent. Repeat FLT3-ITD PCR peripheral blood: NEGATIVE. Plan: Continue routine surveillance. Next bone marrow biopsy at 2 years post-transplant per protocol. Transition to survivorship care beginning. Late effects monitoring: DEXA scan ordered (bone density), ophthalmology referral for cataract screening, endocrine evaluation for thyroid function and gonadal assessment. Psychosocial support referral offered; patient declines, reports good adjustment. Prognosis discussion: given MRD-negative CR1 prior to transplant, full donor chimerism, and now 12 months in remission, prognosis is favorable with estimated 2-year DFS approximately 50-60% for this ELN adverse-risk group.

documents.text:
OUTPATIENT BMT CLINIC FOLLOW-UP NOTE
Department of Hematology/Oncology – Blood and Marrow Transplant Program

**CHIEF COMPLAINT**
Routine post-allogeneic stem cell transplant follow-up visit, day +45. Patient presents for CBC review, clinical assessment of engraftment, and evaluation of new skin and oral symptoms.

---

**HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS**

Mr. Marcus T. Delgado is a 48-year-old male high school science teacher with a diagnosis of de novo acute myeloid leukemia (AML), NOS, FAB M1 subtype, harboring a FLT3-ITD with high allelic ratio (AR 0.87) and a co-occurring DNMT3A R882H mutation, classified as ELN 2022 adverse-risk disease. He presented approximately 10-11 weeks ago with a 3-week history of progressive fatigue, pallor, gingival hypertrophy with spontaneous bleeding, and occasional epistaxis. His presenting WBC was 84,000/uL with 62% peripheral blasts and a bone marrow blast burden of 78%. Normal karyotype was established by conventional G-banding; no recurrent cytogenetic abnormalities were identified.

He received 7+3 induction chemotherapy with cytarabine 100 mg/m2/day as a continuous IV infusion over days 1-7 and daunorubicin 60 mg/m2/day IV bolus on days 1, 2, and 3, with midostaurin 50 mg PO twice daily on days 8-21 (per RATIFY protocol). His induction course was complicated by febrile neutropenia (blood cultures ultimately negative, responding to cefepime), grade 2 oral mucositis managed with magic mouthwash and dietary modification, grade 2 nausea and vomiting requiring a brief 5-day hold of midostaurin, and grade 1 alopecia. ANC recovery to >1,000/uL was achieved on day 28 of induction.

Day 28-30 bone marrow biopsy confirmed complete morphologic remission (CR1): markedly hypocellular marrow consistent with post-chemotherapy aplasia, less than 5% blasts by morphology, normal karyotype 46,XY on repeat cytogenetics, and FLT3-ITD PCR by molecular testing returned negative. Multiparameter flow cytometry MRD (10-color panel, sensitivity 10^-4) was also negative, with no detectable leukemia-associated immunophenotype at less than 0.01% of cellularity. Post-induction CT imaging demonstrated resolution of the hepatosplenomegaly seen at diagnosis.

Given the ELN adverse-risk designation driven by high-allelic-ratio FLT3-ITD and DNMT3A R882H, the decision was made to proceed directly to allogeneic hematopoietic stem cell transplantation (allo-HSCT) in CR1 without intervening consolidation chemotherapy, in order to minimize time off CR and avoid potential MRD conversion. A 10/10 HLA-matched unrelated male donor, CMV seropositive, was identified through the NMDP. Pre-transplant workup was notable for EF 60% on echocardiogram, FEV1 88% and DLCO 82% predicted on PFTs, normal renal and hepatic function, and CMV/EBV/HSV/VZV seropositivity with negative HIV, Hepatitis B surface antigen, and Hepatitis C antibody.

Myeloablative conditioning was administered approximately 9 weeks after initial diagnosis, consisting of fludarabine 30 mg/m2 IV daily on days -6 through -2 and busulfan 3.2 mg/kg IV daily on days -5 through -2 (FluBu4 regimen), with busulfan pharmacokinetic monitoring and dosing targeted to an AUC of 5,000-6,000 uM*min/day. Seizure prophylaxis with levetiracetam was administered throughout busulfan dosing. GVHD prophylaxis consisted of tacrolimus starting day -3 (target trough 10-15 ng/mL) plus short-course Trexall (MTX 15 mg/m2 on day +1; 10 mg/m2 on days +3, +6, +11).

G-CSF-mobilized peripheral blood stem cells from the 10/10 MUD were infused on day 0, with a total CD34+ cell dose of 5.8 x 10^6/kg, which is an excellent graft product.

The peri-transplant course (days 0 through +30) was notable for: febrile neutropenia on day +8 (blood cultures negative, resolved with cefepime within 48 hours); grade 2 oral mucositis from day +6 requiring IV morphine PCA for pain and 8 days of supplemental tube feeds; and grade 1 nausea managed with ondansetron throughout conditioning. No sinusoidal obstruction syndrome/VOD occurred (bilirubin remained below 2 mg/dL, no weight gain, no right upper quadrant pain). Tacrolimus-related mild tremors (grade 1) were managed with dose adjustment. Creatinine rose to 1.4 mg/dL (grade 1, attributed to tacrolimus nephrotoxicity) and was managed with dose reduction. Neutrophil engraftment was documented on day +14 (ANC >500 for 3 consecutive days) and platelet engraftment on day +19 (>20,000 without transfusion support).

Day +30 bone marrow biopsy showed hypercellular to normocellular marrow with trilineage hematopoiesis, no morphologic evidence of AML, FLT3-ITD PCR negative, and near-complete donor chimerism: 97% donor whole blood, 99% donor on myeloid (CD33+) fraction, and 91% donor on T-cell (CD3+) fraction.

Now at day +45, Mr. Delgado returns to clinic with complaints of dry, mildly scaly skin on both hands and mild sensitivity of the mouth. He denies fever, diarrhea, abdominal pain, or eye dryness. He is eating without significant difficulty. Energy is improving but he remains fatigued (he describes it as roughly 60-70% of his usual baseline). He is staying at home and not yet back to work, but is doing light household activities. He has been compliant with all oral medications including tacrolimus, acyclovir, trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole, and fluconazole. He denies any missed doses.

---

**REVIEW OF SYSTEMS**

Constitutional: Persistent mild fatigue, no fever, no night sweats, weight stable (no significant gain or loss).
Skin/Integument: Bilateral palmar erythema with scaling; no blistering, no ulceration, no tight or woody skin. No new rashes on trunk or extremities.
Head/Eyes/Ears/Nose/Throat: Mild oral sensitivity, especially to spicy or acidic foods; notes erythema and mild roughness of inner cheeks bilaterally. No dry eyes, no blurred vision, no photophobia.
Cardiopulmonary: No dyspnea, no cough, no chest pain, no palpitations.
Gastrointestinal: Appetite improving; no nausea, no vomiting, no diarrhea, no abdominal cramping, no melena. Bowel movements normal (1 per day).
Genitourinary: No dysuria, no hematuria. Mild decrease in libido noted but deferred for now.
Musculoskeletal: Mild generalized deconditioning; no joint swelling, no muscle cramping or weakness.
Neurologic: No headache, no focal neurologic symptoms. Mild hand tremor (left > right) noted on last visit, has improved after tacrolimus dose adjustment.
Endocrine/Metabolic: No heat/cold intolerance, no polyuria/polydipsia.
Psychiatric: Mood "okay"; patient reports some anxiety about surveillance results, which is understandable. No significant depression. Coping reasonably well.

---

**PAST MEDICAL HISTORY**
See prior notes. In brief:
- De novo AML, NOS (FAB M1), FLT3-ITD high allelic ratio (AR 0.87), DNMT3A R882H, ELN adverse-risk — diagnosed approximately 3 months ago
- Hypertension, well-controlled on lisinopril (held perioperatively, to be restarted per primary care once BP and renal function stable)
- No prior hematologic conditions
- No prior malignancy

**SOCIAL HISTORY**
Non-smoker, no alcohol use, no illicit drug use. High school science teacher, currently on medical leave. Lives at home with wife and two teenage children. Has good social support structure. No pets with fur or feathers (important for immune-compromised status post-transplant).

**FAMILY HISTORY**
Maternal uncle with colon cancer diagnosed at age 62. No family history of hematologic malignancy, myelodysplastic syndrome, aplastic anemia, or inherited bone marrow failure syndromes.

**ALLERGIES**
NKDA (no known drug allergies). No drug reactions reported with induction chemotherapy or conditioning regimen to date.

**MEDICATIONS (Current)**
1. Tacrolimus (Prograf) — dose adjusted per trough level, current target trough 8-10 ng/mL (tapering from initial 10-15 ng/mL target)
2. Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole (Bactrim) SS — one tablet PO Monday/Wednesday/Friday (PCP prophylaxis)
3. Acyclovir 400 mg PO twice daily (HSV/VZV prophylaxis)
4. Fluconazole 100 mg PO daily (antifungal prophylaxis)
5. Ursodiol 300 mg PO twice daily (hepatic protection, anti-cholestatic, per BMT protocol)
6. Ondansetron 4 mg PO as needed (nausea)
7. Lisinopril — currently held (peri-transplant renal monitoring; to be re-evaluated at this visit)

*2. Mild Chronic GVHD — Skin and Oral Mucosa (NIH 2014 Consensus Criteria: Mild)*
The patient has developed bilateral palmar erythema with fine scaling (skin score 1, <18% BSA, no sclerosis or deeper involvement) and lichenoid changes of the bilateral buccal mucosa with mild symptoms (oral score 1, able to eat, no significant limitation). There is no GI involvement (no diarrhea, no malabsorption), no ocular involvement (no sicca symptoms), no pulmonary GVHD, and no sclerodermatous changes. By NIH consensus criteria, this is classified as mild chronic GVHD involving 2 organs (skin and oral), both at score 1, with no organ at score 2 or above — qualifying as mild overall chronic GVHD. The mild transaminase elevation (AST 42, ALT 48) is borderline and could reflect early hepatic cGVHD contribution, though is also potentially attributable to tacrolimus or ursodiol context; it does not rise to a hepatic cGVHD organ score of 1 at this time per criteria (no bilirubin elevation), but will be monitored closely.

*3. Mildly Elevated Transaminases — Monitoring*
AST 42 and ALT 48 U/L represent grade 1 elevations. Differential includes early hepatic cGVHD, tacrolimus hepatotoxicity, or residual medication effect from ursodiol. Total bilirubin and alkaline phosphatase remain normal, arguing against significant biliary involvement. Continue current hepatic monitoring. Repeat LFTs at next clinic visit in 2 weeks. If transaminases worsen or bilirubin rises, will re-evaluate for hepatic GVHD more formally and consider liver biopsy if clinically indicated.

Albumin 3.6 g/dL reflects mild nutritional deficit, expected at this stage. Patient is eating reasonably well. Encourage a high-protein, calorie-adequate diet. Continue multivitamin. No need for IV supplementation at this point. Referral to BMT dietitian if appetite decreases.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:

Mr. Delgado is a 48-year-old non-smoking male with a history of well-controlled hypertension who was diagnosed approximately six weeks ago with de novo acute myeloid leukemia (AML), NOS — specifically AML without maturation (FAB M1). His presentation was notable for a three-week prodrome of progressive fatigue, pallor, easy bruising, spontaneous gingival bleeding with gingival hypertrophy, and occasional epistaxis. At the time of diagnosis, his presenting CBC was remarkable for a WBC of 84,000/uL, hemoglobin of 7.2 g/dL, and platelets of 28,000/uL, with 62% circulating blasts on peripheral smear. Bone marrow biopsy confirmed markedly hypercellular marrow with 78% blasts. Karyotype was normal (46,XY diploid) on conventional G-band cytogenetics. Comprehensive myeloid NGS panel (283-gene) identified a FLT3 internal tandem duplication (ITD) at the exon 14-15 junction with a high allelic ratio of 0.87 (VAF 47%), and a co-occurring DNMT3A p.R882H mutation (VAF 42%); NPM1 was wild-type. Per ELN 2022 criteria, he was stratified as adverse-risk AML, largely driven by the FLT3-ITD with high allelic ratio in the absence of NPM1 co-mutation. No cytogenetic high-risk features, KMT2A rearrangement, TP53 mutation, or monosomal karyotype were identified. CT staging at diagnosis showed mild hepatosplenomegaly consistent with leukemic infiltration; no extramedullary disease or chloroma was identified.

He was admitted and initiated on induction chemotherapy with the 7+3 regimen: cytarabine 100 mg/m2/day by continuous IV infusion days 1-7, daunorubicin 60 mg/m2 IV bolus days 1-3, and midostaurin 50 mg PO BID days 8-21 per the RATIFY protocol. His hospital course was complicated by expected post-induction febrile neutropenia (ANC nadir 0, day 14; blood cultures negative, responded to empiric cefepime), grade 2 oral mucositis, and grade 2 nausea/vomiting. Midostaurin was briefly held for 5 days due to nausea and then restarted at the same dose. ANC recovery to >1,000/uL was achieved on day 28.

Day 28-30 remission assessment bone marrow biopsy confirmed complete morphologic remission (CR1): marrow markedly hypocellular with trilineage recovery underway, blasts <5% by morphology. FLT3-ITD PCR was NEGATIVE. Multiparameter flow cytometry MRD (10-color panel, sensitivity 10^-4) was NEGATIVE — no leukemia-associated immunophenotype detected. Repeat CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis showed resolution of the prior hepatosplenomegaly with no evidence of extramedullary disease. Peripheral blood counts at day 30-32 follow-up: WBC 3.8 k/uL, ANC 1,820/uL, Hgb 10.4 g/dL, platelets 82,000/uL, continuing to recover.

Given the ELN adverse-risk profile (FLT3-ITD high allelic ratio, DNMT3A R882H co-mutation, NPM1 wild-type), allogeneic HSCT in CR1 is strongly recommended per current guidelines and institutional practice. HLA typing was submitted at diagnosis (10-locus high-resolution typing: HLA-A, B, C, DRB1, DQB1). A 10/10 HLA-allele level matched unrelated donor (MUD) has been identified through the NMDP registry — a CMV-seropositive male donor. CHIEF COMPLAINT

Mr. Delgado is a 48-year-old male with de novo AML (FLT3-ITD high allelic ratio, DNMT3A R882H, ELN adverse-risk) who presents to the outpatient BMT clinic for his day +45 post-allogeneic stem cell transplant follow-up visit. HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Mr. Delgado was diagnosed with acute myeloid leukemia approximately 3.5 months ago. His presenting features included leukocytosis with a WBC of 84,000/uL, significant anemia (hemoglobin 7.2 g/dL), thrombocytopenia (platelets 28,000/uL), and gingival hypertrophy with spontaneous bleeding. Bone marrow biopsy at diagnosis confirmed 78% blasts with an immunophenotype consistent with AML (CD34+, CD117+, MPO+). NGS revealed FLT3-ITD with a high allelic ratio of 0.87 and a co-occurring DNMT3A R882H mutation; cytogenetics showed a normal diploid karyotype (46,XY). He was classified as ELN 2022 adverse risk.

He was treated with 7+3 induction chemotherapy (cytarabine continuous infusion days 1-7, daunorubicin days 1-3) plus midostaurin 50 mg twice daily on days 8-21, consistent with the RATIFY regimen. Induction was complicated by febrile neutropenia (cultures negative), grade 2 oral mucositis requiring supportive care, and nausea/vomiting necessitating a brief 5-day hold of midostaurin, which was subsequently restarted. ANC recovery occurred on day +28 of induction.

Day 28-30 remission assessment bone marrow biopsy confirmed complete morphologic remission (CR1) with fewer than 5% blasts, normal karyotype, and FLT3-ITD negativity by PCR. Multiparameter flow cytometry MRD was negative (sensitivity 10^-4). Given ELN adverse-risk disease and the robust MRD-negative CR1, the decision was made to proceed directly to allogeneic hematopoietic stem cell transplantation without additional consolidation chemotherapy.

Pre-transplant evaluation was completed approximately 6 weeks after induction. Cardiac function was confirmed adequate (EF 60%), pulmonary function tests showed FEV1 88% predicted and DLCO 82% predicted, and organ function was otherwise within normal limits. A 10/10 HLA-matched unrelated male donor (CMV seropositive) was identified through the NMDP.

Approximately 9 weeks after his initial AML diagnosis, the patient was admitted for myeloablative conditioning with the FluBu4 regimen: fludarabine 30 mg/m2 IV daily on days -6 through -2 and busulfan 3.2 mg/kg IV daily on days -5 through -2, with pharmacokinetic monitoring and dose adjustment to target AUC. Seizure prophylaxis with levetiracetam was administered throughout busulfan dosing. GVHD prophylaxis consisted of tacrolimus (started day -3) and short-course methotrexate (15 mg/m2 day +1, 10 mg/m2 days +3, +6, +11). G-CSF-mobilized peripheral blood stem cells were infused on day 0 with a CD34+ cell dose of 5.8 x 10^6/kg.

The peri-transplant course was notable for febrile neutropenia on day +8 (ANC nadir of 0, cultures negative, responded to cefepime in 48 hours), grade 2 oral mucositis requiring IV morphine PCA and approximately 8 days of supplemental tube feeds, and grade 1 tacrolimus-related tremors that improved with dose adjustment. Mild creatinine elevation (peak 1.4 mg/dL) was attributed to tacrolimus nephrotoxicity and managed with dose reduction. Importantly, there was no sinusoidal obstruction syndrome. Neutrophil engraftment was achieved on day +14 and platelet engraftment on day +19.

Day +30 bone marrow biopsy confirmed engraftment with trilineage hematopoiesis, whole blood chimerism 97% donor (CD33+ myeloid chimerism 99% donor, CD3+ T-cell chimerism 91% donor), and FLT3-ITD PCR negative. There was no morphologic evidence of residual or recurrent AML.

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

Mr. Delgado is a 48-year-old male with de novo AML (FLT3-ITD, high allelic ratio; DNMT3A R882H co-mutation; ELN 2022 adverse risk, normal karyotype) who achieved MRD-negative complete remission after one cycle of 7+3 plus midostaurin induction and subsequently underwent myeloablative FluBu4 conditioning followed by 10/10 matched unrelated donor peripheral blood stem cell transplantation. He is now at day +45 post-transplant.

His transplant course has been overall favorable: he engrafted appropriately (neutrophils day +14, platelets day +19), achieved near-complete donor chimerism by day +30 bone marrow assessment, and has maintained FLT3-ITD PCR negativity. He is now presenting with features consistent with early mild chronic graft-versus-host disease.

1. MILD CHRONIC GVHD - SKIN AND ORAL MUCOSA
The patient's skin findings (bilateral palmar and digital erythema with fine scaling, estimated <10% BSA, non-sclerotic) and oral findings (bilateral buccal lichenoid changes with Wickham striae, no ulcerations, tolerating full oral diet) are consistent with mild chronic GVHD by NIH 2014 consensus criteria. Onset at day +45 in the context of peripheral blood stem cell graft and ongoing tacrolimus taper is a typical presentation. There are mildly elevated transaminases (AST 42, ALT 48) with normal bilirubin and alkaline phosphatase; this may represent a subclinical hepatic cGVHD contribution, though other etiologies (including medication effect) are possible and this does not meet criteria for hepatic cGVHD scoring. No gastrointestinal, pulmonary, ocular, joint, or fascial involvement.

NIH global cGVHD scoring: Mild (two or fewer organs with score 1, no organ with score 3, no impact on daily activities).

Plan:
- Initiate topical corticosteroid therapy for skin cGVHD: triamcinolone acetonide 0.1% ointment applied to affected palms and digits twice daily. Counsel on proper application, moisturization, and avoidance of skin trauma.
- Initiate topical therapy for oral cGVHD: magic mouthwash (lidocaine/diphenhydramine/Maalox) as needed for symptomatic relief; additionally prescribe budesonide 0.3 mg/mL oral rinse (swish and spit) three times daily for lichenoid changes.
- Continue tacrolimus at current taper per protocol; do not accelerate taper given new cGVHD.
- Do not initiate systemic corticosteroids at this time given mild disease and response expected with topical therapy.
- Monitor LFTs closely; if transaminases worsen or bilirubin rises, will need to reconsider hepatic cGVHD and evaluate further (ursodiol, possible liver biopsy).
- Patient educated on signs of cGVHD progression: worsening skin tightening, oral ulcerations, eye dryness/pain, diarrhea, jaundice. Instructed to call clinic if symptoms worsen.

2. AML REMISSION STATUS - MONITORING
Patient remains in complete morphologic remission with MRD negativity and full donor chimerism established at day +30. FLT3-ITD PCR from peripheral blood today is negative. LDH is normal. CBC shows adequate engraftment with counts recovering appropriately (ANC 3,100, Hgb 11.8, platelets 142,000).

Plan:
- Continue quarterly FLT3-ITD molecular monitoring by PCR (peripheral blood).
- Next formal bone marrow biopsy with chimerism and MRD assessment planned at day +100 per protocol.
- Remain vigilant for signs of relapse: unexplained cytopenias, constitutional symptoms, or rising FLT3-ITD PCR signal.

3. IMMUNOSUPPRESSION AND INFECTIOUS PROPHYLAXIS
Tacrolimus taper is proceeding (current trough 8.2 ng/mL; target 5-8 ng/mL over next 4-6 weeks). Mild ongoing nephrotoxicity (creatinine 1.1 mg/dL, above his baseline of 0.9 mg/dL) is being monitored. Hypomagnesemia is a recognized tacrolimus effect; continue oral supplementation.

CMV and EBV PCR surveillance both undetectable today. Will continue biweekly CMV PCR monitoring through day +100 given CMV-seropositive donor/recipient pair.

Plan:
- Continue trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole for PCP prophylaxis (MWF dosing).
- Continue acyclovir for HSV/VZV prophylaxis (to be continued at least until off all immunosuppression).
- Continue fluconazole antifungal prophylaxis (until at least 75 days post-transplant, per institutional protocol).
- Continue CMV PCR monitoring biweekly; if CMV PCR becomes detectable, initiate preemptive therapy per protocol (valganciclovir first-line).
- Recheck BMP in 2 weeks with attention to creatinine trend and magnesium.
- Glucose monitoring: mild elevation today likely tacrolimus effect; recheck fasting glucose; refer to endocrinology if post-transplant diabetes develops.

4. GENERAL RECOVERY AND SUPPORTIVE CARE
Mr. Delgado's functional status is improving (ECOG 1). He has regained 3 of the 7 kg lost during transplant hospitalization and reports improving appetite. His hypertension is being managed with lisinopril (BP 128/78 today, well-controlled).

Plan:
- Encourage continued oral intake and gradual activity as tolerated. Physical therapy referral offered (patient declines at this time, prefers home exercise).
- Counsel on sun avoidance and use of SPF 50+ sunscreen given cGVHD and ongoing immunosuppression (increased skin cancer risk).
- Counsel on infection precautions: avoid crowds, sick contacts; hand hygiene; food safety.
- No live vaccines until 24 months post-transplant and off all immunosuppression.
- Continue lisinopril for hypertension; monitor renal function.
- Next clinic visit in 2-3 weeks; CBC, CMP, tacrolimus level, CMV PCR at next visit.

FOLLOW-UP: Scheduled in 2-3 weeks (approximately day +65 to +70 post-transplant) for reassessment of GVHD response to topical therapy, CBC, chemistries, tacrolimus level, and CMV surveillance.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Marcus T. Delgado is a 48-year-old male with de novo AML (NOS, AML without maturation by FAB/M1 classification) who is now at day +100 following a 10/10 HLA-matched unrelated donor (MUD) allogeneic peripheral blood stem cell transplant. He is here for his scheduled day +100 long-term follow-up evaluation.

Briefly, the patient was diagnosed with AML approximately five months ago following a three-week prodrome of progressive fatigue, pallor, gingival hypertrophy with spontaneous bleeding, and epistaxis. At diagnosis, his WBC was 84,000/uL with 62% circulating blasts. Bone marrow biopsy confirmed 78% involvement. NGS revealed a FLT3-ITD with a high allelic ratio of 0.87 (VAF 47%) and a DNMT3A R882H co-mutation — placing him firmly in ELN 2022 adverse-risk disease. Cytogenetics were diploid, 46,XY, without structural abnormalities or high-risk fusions.

He received standard induction chemotherapy with cytarabine/daunorubicin (7+3) plus midostaurin, completing cycle 1 in the inpatient setting. He experienced expected treatment-related toxicities including febrile neutropenia with an ANC nadir of zero around day 14, grade 2 oral mucositis requiring modified diet, and grade 2 nausea/vomiting that necessitated a brief five-day hold of midostaurin. Cultures were negative throughout. He recovered his ANC to greater than 1,000/uL by day 28. Day 28-30 bone marrow biopsy demonstrated complete morphologic remission with less than 5% blasts and no identifiable residual leukemia, and MRD assessment by 10-color multiparameter flow cytometry was negative at the 10^-4 sensitivity level. Repeat cytogenetics showed 46,XY normal karyotype and FLT3-ITD by PCR was negative.

Given his adverse-risk disease (FLT3-ITD high allelic ratio, DNMT3A R882H co-mutation) and MRD-negative CR1, he proceeded expeditiously to allogeneic transplant without additional consolidation chemotherapy. Pre-transplant evaluation confirmed preserved cardiac function (echo EF 60%), adequate pulmonary reserve (FEV1 88% predicted, DLCO 82% predicted), normal renal and hepatic function, and a favorable infectious disease serologic profile. A 10/10 HLA-matched unrelated donor was identified through NMDP — male, CMV seropositive. He received myeloablative conditioning with fludarabine/busulfan (FluBu4), with busulfan PK monitoring performed and dose adjustments made to achieve the target AUC. GVHD prophylaxis consisted of tacrolimus (started day -3, target trough 10-15 ng/mL) and short-course MTX (MTX 15 mg/m2 day +1, 10 mg/m2 days +3, +6, +11). G-CSF-mobilized peripheral blood stem cells were infused on day 0, with a CD34+ cell dose of 5.8 x 10^6/kg.

The peri-transplant period was complicated by febrile neutropenia (day +8, blood cultures negative, resolved on cefepime), grade 2 oral mucositis requiring IV morphine PCA and supplemental enteral nutritional support for approximately eight days, and grade 1 tacrolimus-related nephrotoxicity (creatinine peak 1.4 mg/dL, managed with dose reduction). Neutrophil engraftment was confirmed on day +14 and platelet engraftment on day +19. He did not develop sinusoidal obstruction syndrome. Day +30 bone marrow biopsy confirmed engraftment with trilineage hematopoiesis, STR chimerism 97% donor (myeloid 99%), and FLT3-ITD PCR negative.

At his day +45 visit, he developed mild chronic GVHD affecting the skin (grade 1 erythema and scaling of bilateral palms, less than 18% BSA, no scleroderma) and oral mucosa (grade 1 lichenoid changes of buccal mucosa bilaterally, able to eat without significant difficulty). He was started on topical triamcinolone ointment 0.1% for the palmar skin involvement and topical budesonide oral rinse plus magic mouthwash for oral symptoms. No systemic corticosteroids were required. Tacrolimus taper was begun with a target trough of 5-8 ng/mL. During follow-up between days +60 and +120, mild transaminase elevations were noted (AST 42, ALT 48) with bilirubin within normal limits, felt to be a possible minor hepatic cGVHD contribution, though not meeting criteria for hepatic GVHD organ score. Pulmonary function tests were stable (FEV1 91% predicted), and there was no GI or ocular GVHD. The patient continued to show clinical improvement in GVHD symptoms on the ongoing topical regimen.

Today, at day +100, the patient reports:

- Returned to part-time work as a high school science teacher approximately two weeks ago — tolerating this well
- Residual mild dryness and occasional mild scaling of bilateral hands, though substantially improved since day +45

- Denies dysuria or hematuria
- Sleep improving; no significant insomnia

- No new skin rashes beyond previously known palmar involvement
- No joint stiffness or mobility limitations

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

1. AML IN COMPLETE REMISSION, DAY +100 POST-MUD ALLO-HSCT

Marcus continues to do exceptionally well at day +100 after his 10/10 MUD allogeneic peripheral blood stem cell transplant. His CBC is fully normalized with a WBC of 6.8k, ANC 4,200, hemoglobin 13.1, and platelets 198k. This is a gratifying hematologic picture. FLT3-ITD PCR from peripheral blood is again negative, consistent with molecular remission being maintained. CMV and EBV PCRs are both undetectable, which is reassuring given the CMV-seropositive donor/CMV-seropositive recipient pairing. LFTs have improved further from the mild transaminase elevations seen at the prior visit (AST now 34, ALT 38, bili normal) — this normalization is in keeping with settling of any hepatic GVHD contribution that was suspected earlier. Creatinine has recovered to 1.0 mg/dL, near his pre-transplant baseline, following the tacrolimus-related grade 1 nephrotoxicity during the peri-transplant period.

Day +100 bone marrow biopsy is planned and will be performed today per protocol (order placed).
- Specimens: bilateral posterior iliac crest core biopsies and aspirate
- Studies requested: morphology and blast %, reticulin stain, CD34/CD117 IHC, STR-based chimerism (whole blood, CD3+, CD33+), FLT3-ITD PCR (sensitivity 10^-4), MRD by 10-color multiparameter flow cytometry (sensitivity 10^-4), DNMT3A R882H ddPCR
- Results expected within 7-10 business days; will be reviewed at next scheduled visit or communicated by phone/portal

Plan also includes scheduling bone marrow biopsy results review visit in approximately 2 weeks.

2. MILD CHRONIC GVHD — SKIN AND ORAL, IMPROVING

His mild chronic GVHD continues on a favorable trajectory. Skin manifestations (bilateral palmar erythema and scaling) are substantially improved — no active erythema appreciated on exam today, only mild residual fine scaling. Oral lichenoid changes of buccal mucosa persist but are faint and asymptomatic at rest, with only mild food sensitivity noted. Liver function tests have normalized, reducing prior concern for a hepatic GVHD contribution. No GI, pulmonary, ocular, or musculoskeletal GVHD has emerged.

Global NIH chronic GVHD score: Mild (skin score 1 decreasing, oral score 1 stable-to-improving, no other organ involvement).

- Continue topical triamcinolone 0.1% ointment to bilateral palms as needed (taper to once daily given improvement)
- Continue budesonide oral rinse swish and spit twice daily and magic mouthwash PRN
- Tacrolimus taper: currently 6.2 ng/mL; continue taper with target discontinuation by month 6-9 post-transplant per plan. Next reduction step in approximately 3-4 weeks pending stable GVHD trajectory. He was counseled that discontinuing immunosuppression is the long-term goal and that sustained absence of systemic immunosuppression will signal durability of the graft-versus-leukemia effect.
- No systemic corticosteroids needed at this time
- Hepatic panel to be repeated in 4 weeks to confirm sustained normalization

3. INFECTIOUS PROPHYLAXIS / CMV MONITORING

CMV PCR undetectable. EBV undetectable. He remains on the standard post-transplant prophylaxis regimen:
- TMP-SMX 1 SS tablet three times weekly for PCP prophylaxis (continue until at least 6 months off immunosuppression, approximately through month 12-15 post-transplant pending tacrolimus discontinuation)
- Acyclovir 400 mg BID for HSV/VZV prophylaxis (continue through at least month 12)
- Fluconazole 200 mg daily for antifungal prophylaxis (continue through month 6, then reassess)
- CMV PCR monitoring: continue every 2 weeks while on tacrolimus; may space to monthly once tacrolimus discontinued
- EBV PCR monitoring: every 2 weeks; continue current frequency given CMV-seropositive donor

4. HYPERTENSION

Blood pressure today 128/78 mmHg. Well-controlled on lisinopril 10 mg daily. Continue current dose. Creatinine normalized to 1.0 mg/dL — appropriate to continue ACE inhibitor. Monitor electrolytes in the context of concurrent tacrolimus use (tacrolimus can cause hyperkalemia; potassium today 4.1 mEq/L, acceptable).

5. RETURN TO WORK AND QUALITY OF LIFE

Mr. Delgado has returned to part-time teaching, which is a remarkable milestone at day +100. We discussed gradual activity progression. He was counseled about avoiding large crowded gatherings while still on immunosuppression, hand hygiene, and food safety precautions (avoid unpasteurized products, raw fish, etc.) which remain relevant while on tacrolimus. He should wear SPF 30+ sunscreen and protective clothing given post-transplant increased skin cancer risk associated with chronic GVHD and ongoing immunosuppression.

He has mild ongoing cognitive fogginess, which is expected in the post-transplant recovery period and is typically self-limiting over 6-12 months. Neuropsychological testing may be offered if symptoms persist or interfere significantly with work.

6. LATE EFFECTS MONITORING / PREVENTIVE CARE

- DEXA scan: ordered today for baseline bone density assessment (chronic corticosteroid exposure during transplant course, tacrolimus-related effects, and gonadal suppression are all contributing risk factors for bone loss). If osteopenia is identified, bisphosphonate therapy will be considered.
- Ophthalmology referral: placed for cataract screening and assessment for ocular cGVHD (anterior eye exam). Cataracts are common after busulfan-containing conditioning.
- Endocrine referral: placed for thyroid function assessment and evaluation of gonadal function given myeloablative conditioning. Testosterone and FSH/LH to be drawn at next visit if endocrine referral not yet seen. TSH will be drawn today.
- Cholesterol/lipid panel: ordered (tacrolimus, corticosteroids, and general post-transplant metabolic risk).
- Secondary malignancy risk counseling: provided. He understands the long-term increased risk of cutaneous squamous cell carcinoma (particularly in the setting of ongoing immunosuppression and GVHD) and therapy-related myeloid neoplasms, and will continue routine dermatologic surveillance and hematologic monitoring.
- Annual dental visit: discussed importance of regular dental care given prior oral GVHD, history of gingival hypertrophy, and ongoing tacrolimus-related gingival effects.

8. FOLLOW-UP

Next visit: in approximately 2 weeks to review day +100 bone marrow biopsy and chimerism results. Subsequently, routine outpatient follow-up every 4-6 weeks while on tacrolimus, with planned transition to quarterly surveillance visits once tacrolimus is discontinued.

CHIEF COMPLAINT

Mr. Delgado is a 49-year-old male with a history of de novo acute myeloid leukemia (AML, FAB M1, FLT3-ITD high allelic ratio, DNMT3A R882H, ELN 2022 adverse risk) who presents today for his annual post-transplant surveillance visit, approximately 12-13 months following matched unrelated donor (MUD) allogeneic hematopoietic stem cell transplantation (allo-HSCT). He is doing well overall and has no new complaints.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Mr. Delgado was diagnosed with de novo AML approximately 14 months ago after presenting with a 3-week history of progressive fatigue, pallor, gingival hypertrophy with spontaneous bleeding, and epistaxis. At diagnosis, his WBC was markedly elevated at 84,000/uL with 62% circulating blasts. Bone marrow biopsy confirmed AML with 78% blasts, normal diploid karyotype (46,XY), and NGS identified a FLT3-ITD with a high allelic ratio of 0.87, co-occurring with a DNMT3A R882H mutation — conferring ELN 2022 adverse-risk classification.

He received standard 7+3 induction (cytarabine 100 mg/m2/day continuous infusion days 1-7 and daunorubicin 60 mg/m2 IV days 1-3) with midostaurin 50 mg PO BID days 8-21 per the RATIFY protocol. His course was complicated by febrile neutropenia (cultures negative), grade 2 oral mucositis, and grade 2 nausea requiring temporary midostaurin hold. He achieved complete morphologic remission after a single cycle of induction, with MRD negativity by 10-color multiparameter flow cytometry (sensitivity 10^-4) and FLT3-ITD PCR negativity confirmed on the day +28-30 bone marrow assessment.

Given the adverse-risk biology, he proceeded directly to myeloablative allogeneic transplant in CR1 without intervening consolidation, approximately 9 weeks from diagnosis. A 10/10 HLA-matched unrelated donor was identified through the NMDP (male, CMV seropositive). Conditioning was FluBu4 (fludarabine 30 mg/m2/day x5 days, busulfan 3.2 mg/kg/day x4 days IV with pharmacokinetic targeting). GVHD prophylaxis was tacrolimus plus short-course MTX. Peripheral blood stem cells were infused on day 0 (CD34+ dose 5.8 x 10^6/kg). Engraftment was achieved (neutrophil day +14, platelet day +19), complicated by grade 2 mucositis requiring temporary PCA morphine and supplemental tube feeds, febrile neutropenia (blood cultures negative), and mild tacrolimus-associated nephrotoxicity (grade 1, creatinine peak 1.4 mg/dL, responsive to dose reduction).

Post-transplant course has been notable for mild chronic GVHD involving skin (bilateral palms, erythema and scaling, NIH score 1) and oral mucosa (lichenoid changes of bilateral buccal mucosa, NIH score 1, no symptoms interfering with eating), which was managed with topical triamcinolone and budesonide oral rinse respectively. No systemic corticosteroids were ever required. Tacrolimus was successfully tapered and discontinued at approximately 8 months post-transplant without GVHD flare. Follow-up bone marrow biopsy at day +180 confirmed ongoing complete morphologic remission, MRD negativity by flow and digital droplet PCR for DNMT3A R882H, FLT3-ITD PCR negativity, and full donor chimerism (whole blood 99% donor, myeloid 100% donor, T-cell 96% donor). Surveillance CT imaging at the 12-month mark showed no evidence of extramedullary disease or AML recurrence.

At today's visit, the patient reports he has returned to full-time work as a high school science teacher and has fully resumed recreational activities including recreational basketball. He has no new constitutional symptoms, no fever, no night sweats, no unexplained weight loss, no lymphadenopathy, no bone pain, and no new skin or mucosal complaints. Chronic GVHD of skin and oral mucosa has fully resolved clinically. He is not currently on any immunosuppression.

Mr. Delgado continues to do extremely well. He is 12-13 months out from a 10/10 MUD allo-HSCT in CR1, with serially negative MRD by flow cytometry and FLT3-ITD PCR, full donor chimerism confirmed at day +180, and normal CBC and metabolic labs today. The combination of MRD-negative CR1 at transplant, full engraftment and full donor chimerism, and absence of relapse at the 12-month mark is reassuring. For ELN adverse-risk AML transplanted in MRD-negative CR1, the estimated 2-year DFS in published series is approximately 50-60%, and this patient's course to date has been favorable.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Mr. Delgado was diagnosed with de novo AML approximately 13 months ago after presenting with a three-week history of progressive fatigue, pallor, gingival hypertrophy with spontaneous mucosal bleeding, and epistaxis. At presentation, his WBC was markedly elevated at 84,000/uL with 62% circulating blasts, hemoglobin 7.2 g/dL, and platelets 28,000/uL. Bone marrow biopsy confirmed AML with 78% blasts, normal diploid karyotype (46,XY), and next-generation sequencing demonstrated a high allelic ratio FLT3-ITD (VAF 47%, AR 0.87) with a co-occurring DNMT3A R882H mutation (VAF 42%), placing him in the ELN 2022 adverse-risk category. CSF cytology was negative. He had no evidence of extramedullary disease on staging CT, though he had mild hepatosplenomegaly consistent with leukemic infiltration.

He underwent standard 7+3 induction chemotherapy with cytarabine continuous infusion plus daunorubicin, and midostaurin 50 mg PO twice daily on days 8-21 per the RATIFY regimen. Induction was complicated by febrile neutropenia with an ANC nadir of zero (cultures negative), grade 2 oral mucositis, and grade 2 nausea/vomiting requiring a brief midostaurin hold. He achieved complete morphologic remission after a single induction cycle, with MRD negativity confirmed by 10-color multiparameter flow cytometry (sensitivity 10^-4) and FLT3-ITD PCR negativity.

Given his adverse-risk disease, he proceeded expeditiously to allogeneic transplant in CR1. A 10/10 HLA-matched unrelated male donor (CMV seropositive) was identified through the NMDP. He received myeloablative conditioning with fludarabine 30 mg/m2 x 5 days plus busulfan 3.2 mg/kg/day x 4 days (FluBu4) with busulfan PK monitoring and seizure prophylaxis with levetiracetam. GVHD prophylaxis consisted of tacrolimus plus short-course Trexall (days +1, +3, +6, +11). G-CSF-mobilized peripheral blood stem cells were infused on day 0 (CD34+ dose 5.8 x 10^6/kg). The immediate post-transplant course was notable for neutrophil engraftment on day +14, platelet engraftment on day +19, grade 2 oral mucositis requiring IV morphine PCA and supplemental tube feeds for approximately 8 days, and culture-negative febrile neutropenia with prompt response to empiric cefepime. He had no SOS/VOD, no significant hepatotoxicity, and only grade 1 tacrolimus-related nephrotoxicity and tremors, both managed with dose adjustments.

Day +30 bone marrow biopsy demonstrated trilineage engraftment with near-complete donor chimerism (whole blood 97%, myeloid 99%, T-cell 91%), and no morphologic evidence of residual AML. Subsequent evaluations at day +45, day +100, and day +180 all confirmed ongoing complete remission with deepening donor chimerism. By day +180, whole blood chimerism was 99% donor, T-cell chimerism 96%, myeloid chimerism 100% donor. MRD by flow cytometry and FLT3-ITD PCR remained negative through day +180. DNMT3A R882H digital droplet PCR was also negative at day +180.

During months 2-4 post-transplant, he developed mild chronic GVHD (NIH consensus criteria: mild) affecting skin (palmar erythema and scaling, less than 18% BSA, no sclerosis) and oral mucosa (lichenoid changes of buccal mucosa, grade 1). There was no GI, hepatic, pulmonary, or ocular GVHD. He required no systemic corticosteroids; his skin GVHD was managed with topical triamcinolone and his oral GVHD with topical budesonide rinse and magic mouthwash. Tacrolimus was successfully tapered and discontinued at month 8 post-transplant without any flare of GVHD. His skin GVHD fully resolved. Oral GVHD showed significant improvement, with only faint asymptomatic lichenoid buccal changes at his 9-month visit.

At the 12-month post-transplant surveillance CT (performed approximately 2 weeks prior to today's visit), no lymphadenopathy, no pulmonary abnormalities, no hepatosplenomegaly, and no osseous lesions concerning for extramedullary disease were identified. Today's visit represents his formal one-year post-transplant annual review and marks the beginning of the transition to long-term survivorship follow-up.

Since his last visit (approximately 3 months ago), Mr. Delgado reports a full return to his pre-illness functional level. He is back to full-time teaching of high school science and has resumed recreational sports, including weekend tennis. He reports no new constitutional symptoms. He denies fever, night sweats, or unintentional weight loss. He denies any new skin changes, rashes, or pruritus. No new oral symptoms, dysphagia, or xerostomia. CLINICALLY SIGNIFICANT FINDINGS — TIER I / TIER II
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

1. FLT3 — Internal Tandem Duplication (ITD)
   Gene: FLT3 (FMS-like tyrosine kinase 3)
   Alteration Type: Internal tandem duplication (ITD), exon 14–15 juxtamembrane domain junction
   Insertion Size: 51 bp (in-frame)
   Allelic Ratio (AR): 0.87 (HIGH; threshold for high AR: ≥0.5)
   Variant Allele Frequency (VAF) by NGS: 47%
   Confirmation: Capillary electrophoresis fragment analysis performed in parallel — POSITIVE, consistent with above
   Concurrent FLT3 Tyrosine Kinase Domain (TKD) D835 Mutation: NOT DETECTED
   Clinical Significance: FLT3-ITD is a recurrent activating mutation in AML resulting in constitutive activation of FLT3 receptor tyrosine kinase, promoting proliferation and survival of leukemic blasts. High allelic ratio (AR ≥0.5) is an independent adverse prognostic factor. Per ELN 2022 guidelines, FLT3-ITD — regardless of NPM1 co-mutation status — at high allelic ratio confers ADVERSE RISK. The absence of NPM1 co-mutation further solidifies adverse-risk classification. FLT3-ITD is a direct therapeutic target: midostaurin (Rydapt) is FDA-approved in combination with 7+3 induction chemotherapy for FLT3-mutated AML (RATIFY trial). Gilteritinib (Xospata) and sorafenib are available for relapsed/refractory settings and post-transplant maintenance consideration.
   Tier: I (Strong clinical significance — established biomarker with direct therapeutic and prognostic implications)

2. DNMT3A — Missense Mutation
   Gene: DNMT3A (DNA methyltransferase 3 alpha)
   Alteration: p.Arg882His (R882H); c.2645G>A
   Exon: 23
   Variant Allele Frequency (VAF): 42%
   Zygosity: Heterozygous
   dbSNP: rs147001633
   ClinVar: Pathogenic
   COSMIC: COSM52947 (highly recurrent hotspot in AML/MDS)
   Clinical Significance: DNMT3A R882H is the most common hotspot mutation in DNMT3A, occurring in approximately 15–20% of de novo AML cases. It acts as a dominant-negative mutation, impairing the homotetrameric structure of the DNMT3A complex and reducing genome-wide DNA methylation, particularly at hematopoietic stem cell gene loci. DNMT3A R882H is strongly associated with clonal hematopoiesis (CHIP) and is frequently co-mutated with FLT3-ITD and NPM1. In the present case, the co-occurrence of DNMT3A R882H with FLT3-ITD (high AR) in the absence of NPM1 mutation represents a well-described high-risk AML genotype. DNMT3A mutations are associated with persistence as clonal hematopoiesis after therapy and may serve as a substrate for relapse. The VAF of 42% is consistent with a founder or early clonal event. No currently approved targeted therapies exist directed at DNMT3A R882H, although hypomethylating agent-based regimens (azacitidine, decitabine) may have increased activity in DNMT3A-mutated AML — relevant for MRD-positive or relapsed/refractory settings.
   Tier: I (Strong clinical significance — established adverse prognostic biomarker in AML)

─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
FINDINGS OF UNCERTAIN SIGNIFICANCE — TIER III
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

3. PTPN11 — Missense Mutation
   Gene: PTPN11 (protein tyrosine phosphatase non-receptor type 11; encodes SHP-2)
   Alteration: p.Glu76Lys (E76K); c.226G>A
   Exon: 3 (N-SH2 domain, autoinhibitory interface)
   Variant Allele Frequency (VAF): 18%
   Zygosity: Heterozygous
   ClinVar: Variant of uncertain significance in myeloid context; established gain-of-function mutation in Noonan syndrome/RAS-pathway leukemias
   Clinical Significance: PTPN11 E76K is a recurrent gain-of-function mutation that activates the RAS/MAPK signaling pathway through constitutive activation of SHP-2. It is the most commonly mutated codon in PTPN11 across hematologic malignancies. The lower VAF (18%) relative to the dominant FLT3-ITD and DNMT3A clones (VAFs 47% and 42%, respectively) suggests this mutation may represent a leukemic subclone arising from the dominant DNMT3A/FLT3-ITD clone, rather than a founding event. PTPN11 mutations in AML have been described in association with transient abnormal myelopoiesis (TAM) and core-binding factor AML; however, isolated PTPN11 mutations in de novo AML without core-binding factor rearrangements are less well characterized. The subclonal nature of this variant is of note for monitoring purposes, as subclonal mutations may expand at relapse. No approved targeted therapies directly address PTPN11 E76K in AML, though MEK inhibitor-based strategies are under investigation. Clinical correlation recommended.
   Tier: III (Uncertain significance — potentially biologically relevant subclonal finding; insufficient evidence for direct therapeutic or risk classification impact)

ACTIONABLE ALTERATIONS AND THERAPEUTIC CONSIDERATIONS
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

FLT3-ITD — Direct Therapeutic Target:
  - INDUCTION: Midostaurin (Rydapt) 50 mg PO twice daily, days 8–21, in combination with standard 7+3 induction chemotherapy (cytarabine + anthracycline) is FDA-approved and guideline-recommended for FLT3-mutated AML (RATIFY trial; Stone et al., NEJM 2017). Applicable to this patient.
  - CONSOLIDATION/MAINTENANCE: Quizartinib (Vanflyta) maintenance post-induction consolidation is FDA-approved for FLT3-ITD–positive AML in patients achieving CR following induction (QuANTUM-First trial); however, patient's high-risk profile and ELN adverse classification make early allogeneic stem cell transplant the preferred curative strategy.
  - RELAPSED/REFRACTORY: Gilteritinib (Xospata) monotherapy is FDA-approved for relapsed/refractory FLT3-mutated AML (ADMIRAL trial). Sorafenib (Nexavar) maintenance post-allogeneic transplant has shown a relapse-free survival benefit in FLT3-ITD–positive AML (SORMAIN, HOVON-232 trials) and should be considered in the post-transplant maintenance setting.

DNMT3A R882H — No Direct FDA-Approved Targeted Therapy:
  - DNMT3A mutations are not currently directly targetable with approved agents. Ongoing investigation of menin inhibitors (revumenib, ziftomenib) in DNMT3A-mutated/NPM1-mutated AML is noteworthy, though the NPM1 wild-type status in this patient may limit direct applicability. DNMT3A R882H status may inform MRD monitoring strategies (digital droplet PCR feasible at this mutation site).
  - Post-transplant MRD monitoring with DNMT3A R882H ddPCR may provide early relapse detection (residual DNMT3A-mutant clones post-transplant are associated with relapse risk).

PTPN11 E76K — No Directly Approved Targeted Therapy; Clinical Trial Consideration:
  - SHP-2 inhibitors (e.g., RMC-4630, TNO155) are under early-phase clinical investigation and may be relevant in the relapsed/refractory setting if this subclone expands.

MRD MONITORING RECOMMENDATIONS (INFORMATIONAL)
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Molecular MRD-trackable targets identified in this specimen:
  1. FLT3-ITD (AR 0.87, 51-bp insertion): Trackable by fragment analysis / capillary electrophoresis or allele-specific PCR. High sensitivity MRD tracking feasible post-induction and post-transplant. Note: FLT3-ITD may be subject to clonal evolution at relapse (ITD size/location change); negative result does not exclude FLT3-ITD–driven relapse with an evolved clone.
  2. DNMT3A p.R882H: Highly trackable by digital droplet PCR (ddPCR) at sensitivity of 10^-5. Useful for long-term post-transplant monitoring; note that DNMT3A mutations may persist as clonal hematopoiesis in donor-derived cells — positive result post-transplant requires chimerism correlation to distinguish residual recipient disease from donor CHIP.
  3. PTPN11 p.E76K: Trackable by ddPCR. Subclonal at diagnosis (VAF 18%); continued monitoring appropriate given potential for subclonal expansion at relapse.

Multiparameter flow cytometry MRD (leukemia-associated immunophenotype, LAIP-based) is also recommended in parallel with molecular MRD at each assessment.

SUMMARY
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Comprehensive myeloid NGS (283-gene panel) performed on diagnostic bone marrow aspirate from a 48-year-old male with newly diagnosed de novo AML (FAB M1, normal karyotype 46,XY) reveals:

  PATHOGENIC / LIKELY PATHOGENIC (Tier I):
    1. FLT3 ITD, exon 14–15 junctional, 51-bp in-frame insertion, allelic ratio 0.87 (HIGH), VAF 47% — ELN 2022 adverse-risk determinant; direct therapeutic target (midostaurin in induction; gilteritinib/sorafenib for post-transplant or relapsed/refractory settings)
    2. DNMT3A p.R882H (c.2645G>A), VAF 42% — recurrent dominant-negative hotspot mutation; adverse prognostic impact; trackable MRD marker via ddPCR

    3. PTPN11 p.E76K (c.226G>A), VAF 18% — likely leukemic subclone; RAS/MAPK pathway activating mutation; clinical significance in this context uncertain; recommend monitoring

  NO SIGNIFICANT COPY NUMBER ALTERATIONS DETECTED
  NPM1: WILD-TYPE; IDH1/2: WILD-TYPE; TP53: WILD-TYPE; ASXL1: WILD-TYPE

  ELN 2022 RISK: ADVERSE

This genomic profile is consistent with de novo AML driven by a dominant clone carrying FLT3-ITD (high AR) and DNMT3A R882H, with a secondary PTPN11-mutant subclone. The ELN 2022 adverse-risk designation supports treatment with midostaurin-augmented induction chemotherapy followed by allogeneic hematopoietic stem cell transplantation in first complete remission, per current NCCN and ELN guidelines.

METHODS NOTE
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

This assay detects somatic variants (SNVs, small indels, CNVs, and selected fusions) in 283 genes relevant to myeloid malignancies. Germline variants are not systematically distinguished from somatic variants by this assay; germline genetic counseling referral should be considered for variants of potential constitutional significance (e.g., DNMT3A pathogenic variants at VAF ≥45% without clear leukemic expansion context; this specimen VAF of 42% in a high-blast marrow is consistent with somatic/leukemic origin). Variants with VAF <2% are below analytical sensitivity and may not be reported. A normal diploid (46,XY) conventional G-banded karyotype was concurrently performed and is concordant with the absence of large-scale chromosomal alterations by CNV analysis.
